smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
hi Edward, just went through your site, not sure how H2Ocean isn't already on my client list haha.

that's not really why I'm here though. I do Meta ads for ecom brands, and rather than pitch you some BS, I just went ahead and built you a few.

built three ad mockups from H2Ocean's actual products, plus a word-class brand playbook I put together from your site and reviews. it's finished and ready to go on my end.

want me to send it your way?

- Kyle

Kyle 

 Sure send me what you are presenting for Meta Ads, we are interested in the creative content.
 

 Thanks
 Eddie

here you go Edward - 2 ad mockups built from H2Ocean's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
Just set us up for tomorrow. 

 Thanks
 Eddie

copy.tldr: H2Ocean is a 20-year-old natural sea salt mouthwash brand with a strong story and clear post-procedure use case, but no ads running and no reviews on site, so this call is about starting from zero, not fixing a leak.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A natural oral care brand based in Stuart, Florida that has been selling for over 20 years. Their claim to fame is being the original patented sea salt mouthwash. They also serve dental professionals, so there is a real business behind this, not just a Shopify store.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Two products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash in Arctic Ocean Mint and in Fresh Lemon Ice. Both use Red Sea salt, an enzyme called lysozyme, and xylitol. No alcohol, no fluoride, no sugar, no artificial dyes. Sold in 8oz, 16oz, and 64oz sizes. Note: the Fresh Lemon Ice product page shows Sold Out, and the listed price on the mint page is actually $17, not $11. Confirm live prices and stock early.
deep_dive.positioning: A premium, natural alternative to harsh alcohol-based mouthwashes. The gentle, non-burning rinse you trust for healing after dental work, piercings, canker sores, and for daily family use.
deep_dive.who_buys: Health-conscious adults who need a gentle rinse. Two clear groups: people recovering from something specific (dental surgery, oral piercings, canker sores, chemo mouth) and people who just want a natural daily mouthwash for the whole family.
customer_voice: This is great for post-dental work and for oral piercings! It was the only thing that helped my tongue piercing heal. Two thumbs up
strengths: A real, specific use case. People with piercings and post-surgery pain search hard for relief, and this brand solves a sharp pain point. That makes ad angles easy.
20 years in business, a patent, and dental professional backing. That is trust most new ecom brands would kill for.
gaps: No reviews visible on the site. There is huge word-of-mouth trust off-site, but nothing on the page to close a cold visitor from an ad.
No Meta ads found running. There is no existing winner to build on, so month one is pure testing from scratch.

game_plan.headline: Lead with the Arctic Ocean Mint rinse on the piercing and post-dental healing angle, prove one ad works, then widen to daily family use and add the lemon flavor.

phases.goal: Prove that at least one ad can bring in sales at a price that works. We are learning, not scaling.
campaigns: One testing campaign with 2 to 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet, all pointed at the healing and recovery angle
A simple retargeting campaign for people who visit the site but do not buy
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint. We lead with this because it is in stock (the Lemon Ice page shows Sold Out), mint is the most familiar mouthwash flavor to a cold buyer, and it carries the strongest use case in their own customer quote about healing a tongue piercing.
phases.creative: Go live first with the ad built on the piercing and post-dental healing angle, using the real customer line about it being the only thing that helped a tongue piercing heal. This is the sharpest pain point and the one their own reviews prove. Run the other one or two sheet ads against it to see which hook people click.
phases.budget: Around $100 to $150 a day to start.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We find one ad and one audience that brings in sales at a cost we are both happy with. Even a handful of consistent daily orders from one ad is the win here.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and reach new people.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad and audience with a higher daily budget
Test new audiences: dental patients, parents wanting a natural rinse, people avoiding alcohol mouthwash
Fix the biggest gap by adding reviews and testimonials into the ads and the landing page
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint, still the lead, now backed by whichever angle proved best.
phases.creative: Take the winning hook from month one and make two or three fresh versions of it so it does not go stale. Start weaving in more of their real customer language and any social proof Eddie can hand us.
phases.budget: Raise toward $250 to $350 a day only if month one hit its cost target.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad still brings in sales at a good cost at higher spend, and at least one new audience starts working too.

phases.goal: Scale what works and add the second flavor as a new offer.
campaigns: Launch Fresh Lemon Ice as a second product, assuming it is back in stock
Test a bundle or subscribe offer to lift repeat orders, since mouthwash runs out and gets rebought
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Fresh Lemon Ice added here as a fresh angle for people who dislike mint, plus a two-flavor bundle for existing fans.
phases.creative: Introduce the lemon flavor with its own hook. Push the daily-use and family angle harder now that the healing angle is proven. Add a repeat-purchase offer.
phases.budget: Scale on results, not on a fixed number. We only spend more where sales hold.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products selling, steady daily orders, and a repeat-buyer offer in place so revenue is not all one-time.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get roughly 30 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience works

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: This depends on their real order value and margin, which we cannot see yet
estimates.basis: the mint product page lists $17 and the lemon page lists $11, so we cannot pin a reliable average order value from public data alone. We need Eddie to confirm his real average order value and repeat rate before we promise a revenue number. Putting a made-up figure here would be a mistake.

assumptions: Arctic Ocean Mint is in stock and Fresh Lemon Ice will be back in stock by month three.
Their real average order value and margin can support paid ads. At an $11 to $17 single bottle, margin is the thing that decides if this works, so we must confirm it.
Eddie can supply real customer testimonials and photos, since none are on the site right now.
The site can take orders cleanly. We should confirm checkout works before spending a dollar.

questions.question: Are you running any Meta or Facebook ads right now, or have you in the past?
questions.listening_for: We found none running. If they never have, we are starting fresh, which fits the plan. If they stopped, ask why it stopped and what it cost them.

questions.question: Who handles your marketing today, in-house or an agency?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is someone we would work alongside or replace, and how much they already know about paid ads.

questions.question: A single bottle runs between $11 and $17 on your site. What is your typical order value once someone buys, and roughly what is your margin on a bottle?
questions.listening_for: This is the deal-maker. A low order value with thin margin makes paid ads hard. If people buy multiples, the 64oz size, or subscribe, the math gets much better. We cannot promise revenue until we hear this.

questions.question: The last time you tried to grow through ads or online, what happened?
questions.listening_for: Past burns, unrealistic expectations, or a real bad experience with another agency. Shapes how much proof they need from us.

questions.question: I noticed there are no reviews on your product pages, but your customers clearly love this. Do you have reviews or testimonials somewhere we could pull from?
questions.listening_for: Whether social proof exists off-site. If they have a stack of reviews we can add, that closes the biggest gap fast. If not, we build proof into month two.

questions.question: Your Fresh Lemon Ice page shows Sold Out and your mint page lists $17 while I had $11 noted. Can you walk me through current prices and stock?
questions.listening_for: Clear the price and stock confusion before we build anything. If lemon is gone for months, we lead mint only. If pricing is off, our whole plan math changes.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site from an ad, do most people buy, or do they add to cart and leave?
questions.listening_for: If traffic comes but does not convert, the problem is the page or the offer, not the ad. That would change what we fix first.

questions.question: Which brings you more sales today, the healing and recovery buyers or the everyday daily-use buyers?
questions.listening_for: Confirms which angle to lead with. Our plan bets on the healing angle first based on their reviews. If daily use is bigger, we adjust.

questions.question: Do people tend to buy once, or do they come back and rebuy?
questions.listening_for: Mouthwash is a repeat product. A strong repeat rate makes paid ads far safer even at a low first order value, and it justifies a subscribe offer in month three.

questions.question: People actively search for things like sea salt mouthwash, mouthwash for canker sores, and piercing aftercare rinse. Are you showing up on Google when they do?
questions.listening_for: This is a real opening. Their category has clear search demand and a specific problem people type into Google. If they are invisible there, Google ads and SEO are honest next steps, not a forced pitch.

questions.question: You have a big Knowledge Center with clinical studies and articles. Is that content bringing you any traffic from Google today?
questions.listening_for: They already have content most brands lack. If it is not ranking or not turning readers into buyers, there is real SEO value to capture without starting from scratch.

objections.objection: We have been around 20 years and done fine without Facebook ads. Why start now?
objections.answer: That is exactly why this is a good time. You already have the trust and the product most brands spend years building. Ads just put your best story in front of new people who have never heard of you. We start small and prove it works before you spend real money.

objections.objection: Our mouthwash is only around eleven to seventeen dollars. Can ads even pay off at that price?
objections.answer: Fair question, and it is the first thing we need to check together. At a single bottle it is tight. But if people buy the bigger 64oz size, buy both flavors, or rebuy every month, the math works. That is why I want your real order value and repeat rate before I promise anything.

objections.objection: There are no reviews on our site. Won't that hurt the ads?
objections.answer: Honestly, yes, it is the biggest gap I see. Cold buyers need proof. The good news is your customers already say powerful things, like one calling it the only thing that healed their tongue piercing. If you can hand me testimonials, I will build them right into the ads and your page. That alone could lift results a lot.

objections.objection: How do I know your ads will work when you've never run our account?
objections.answer: You don't yet, and that is why month one is a small test, not a big spend. We put a few hundred dollars behind two or three ads, find the one that actually sells, and only then put more money behind it. You are never betting big on an unproven guess.

red_flags.flag: Low price per bottle, listed between $11 and $17.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A single low-priced item can make paid ads unprofitable once you subtract ad cost, product cost, and shipping. We must confirm real order value and margin before promising revenue.

red_flags.flag: No reviews visible anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold ad traffic needs social proof to buy. With nothing on the page, more visitors will leave without buying. We need testimonials from Eddie or the plan gets slower.

red_flags.flag: Fresh Lemon Ice product page shows Sold Out.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot advertise a product people cannot buy. This is why we lead with the mint flavor and hold lemon for later.

red_flags.flag: Price mismatch: mint page shows $17, our notes and the lemon page show $11.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We must get the real, current prices straight before building ads or quoting any numbers, or we look sloppy and our math is wrong.

This is a health product. Meta restricts claims about curing, healing, or treating conditions like canker sores, gum disease, or chemo effects. We must word ads carefully to avoid getting the account flagged.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads. People clearly type in problems this brand solves, like sea salt mouthwash, mouthwash for canker sores, and piercing aftercare rinse. Catching that demand is a natural fit.

SEO on their existing Knowledge Center. They already have clinical studies and articles, which is content most brands do not have. If it is not ranking or converting, there is real value to capture there.
